title_en: Hadiths 1–10: Foundations of Islam, Intention, and Lawful and Unlawful
title_ar: الأحاديث 1–10: أسس الإسلام والنية والحلال والحرام
content_en:
The first ten hadiths of al-Arba'een al-Nawawiyyah establish the foundational principles upon which the entire structure of Islamic practice and ethics rests. Commentators have noted that these ten hadiths alone could serve as a comprehensive introduction to Islamic teaching.

Hadith 1 — the Hadith of Jibreel — defines Islam, Iman, and Ihsan. Islam is submission through the five pillars; Iman is belief in Allah, His angels, His books, His messengers, the Last Day, and divine decree (qadar), both its good and its harm; Ihsan is to worship Allah as though you see Him, and if you cannot see Him, He sees you. Ibn Rajab's commentary on this hadith spans dozens of pages, exploring the theological depths of each element of faith and the relationship between the three levels.

Hadith 2 — 'Islam is built upon five' — lists the five pillars in the famous sequence, providing the structural skeleton of the religion. Hadith 3 — 'Actions are by their intentions' — is perhaps the single most important statement in Islamic ethics, establishing that all deeds are judged by their inner purpose. Ibn Daqiq al-Eid notes that some scholars considered this hadith to be one-third or one-quarter of the entire religion, so many rulings depend on the question of intention.

Hadith 4 on the creation of the human being and divine decree, Hadith 5 on innovation in religion ('every newly invented matter is a bid'ah, and every bid'ah is astray'), and Hadith 6 on the lawful and the unlawful being clear with a gray area between them — 'Whoever falls into the doubtful matters falls into the forbidden, like the shepherd who grazes his flock near a sanctuary' — are three of the most widely applied legal and ethical principles in Islamic scholarship. The commentary on Hadith 6 explores the concept of ihtiyat (precaution) and the boundaries of the permissible with reference to numerous fiqh applications.
